name: Dropcam
short_description: A Wi-Fi camera with cloud recording that made continuous home video monitoring accessible to consumers for the first time.
long_description: Founded in January 2009 by Greg Duffy and Aamir Virani, Dropcam shipped Wi-Fi cameras that streamed live HD video directly to the cloud, requiring no on-site DVR or complex network setup. Users could view a live feed from anywhere via a browser or mobile app, and a paid subscription tier held encrypted recordings for up to a month. Motion and audio detection sent timeline-marked alerts to the phone. By 2013 Dropcam was reportedly processing more uploaded video than YouTube, and Google's Nest Labs acquired the company for $555 million in June 2014. Nest relaunched the hardware as Nest Cam in 2015. Dropcam is the direct ancestor of the modern smart-home camera category, establishing the pattern — plug-in camera, cloud subscription, smartphone notifications — that Ring, Arlo, Nest Cam, and others all followed.
ai_note: Motion and audio detection used signal-processing rules and early computer-vision heuristics; on-device AI was minimal, with intelligence concentrated in the cloud service's activity recognition layer.

images.caption: Dropcam Pro Wi-Fi home monitoring camera (2013).
images.alt_text: A small white cylindrical Dropcam Pro camera with a wide-angle lens, designed to sit on a shelf or be mounted on a wall for home monitoring.

sources.title: Dropcam
sources.access_notes: Covers founding (January 2009), founders Greg Duffy and Aamir Virani, cloud recording service, motion/audio detection, $555M acquisition by Nest Labs in June 2014, and succession by Nest Cam in June 2015.

sources.title: Remote Video Startup Dropcam Raises $30M, Plans To Use The Funding To Better Compete — With Itself
sources.access_notes: Documents Dropcam HD hardware, the cloud platform, 39% paid subscription conversion rate, computer-vision investment, and $47.8M total raised. Describes the company as four years old at July 2013, corroborating a 2009 founding.

sources.title: Google Nest
sources.access_notes: Confirms Dropcam acquired June 2014; Nest Cam announced June 2015 as successor to Dropcam Pro.

interactions.title: Watch the baby from the kitchen
interactions.description: Dropcam was one of the earliest widely-bought workarounds for a dedicated baby monitor: owners positioned the camera at the crib, opened the browser or app from any room, and watched a live HD stream — a use case the company explicitly highlighted.

interactions.title: Rewind the cloud to see who walked past
interactions.description: Because the paid subscription recorded continuously and stored a week or month of footage in the cloud, users could scroll back through a timeline of motion-marked clips to find when the dog escaped, when the nanny arrived, or what caused a crash downstairs.

interactions.title: It flags the loud moments, not just the motion
interactions.description: Audio detection let the camera mark unusually loud events on the recording timeline — a crash, a dog barking, raised voices — so they could be found later instead of being lost in hours of continuous video.

interactions.title: Mount one in the office as a pet cam
interactions.description: Owners working from home or traveling put a Dropcam in the living room purely to watch dogs and cats during the day — a surveillance device reframed as a window into the home's animal life, not its security.

interactions.title: The single camera that replaced the DVR
interactions.description: Prior to Dropcam, home video recording meant buying a separate DVR and running cables to it. One Dropcam on Wi-Fi, plugged into a wall, produced stored cloud footage with zero hardware beyond the camera — a setup consumers found genuinely adoptable at scale, evidenced by the 39% paid-tier conversion rate cited in 2013.

interactions.title: Alert only the moments that matter, skip the rest
interactions.description: Building on the existing motion and audio timeline markers, a future iteration could let owners draw a verbal boundary — 'only alert me if someone enters the living room after 11 pm' — routing the cloud's activity recognition through a natural-language filter instead of a manual schedule.
